title: -Accept Grant Funds from the California Ocean Protection Council’s Senate Bill 1 Sea Level Rise Adaptation Planning Grant Program and contract with Association of Bay Area Governments/San Francisco Estuary Partnership

sections.recommendation:
Adopt a Resolution authorizing the City Manager or their designee to accept the
California Ocean Protection Council’s Senate Bill 1 Sea Level Rise Adaptation Planning
Grant Program funding in the amount of $223,151, and negotiate and execute a
contract and any amendments, extensions, or change orders with the Association of
Bay Area Governments/San Francisco Estuary Partnership, for the East Bay Crescent
Sub-Regional Vulnerability Assessment and Shoreline Adaptation Planning, for the
duration of March 1, 2026 through December 31, 2028.
sections.fiscal_impacts:
There is no match requirement required to receive this grant award. The total funding
available to the City of Berkeley for this project is $223,151, which will be deposited into
Fund 336 (One-Time-Grant). The grant will fund time for City staff positions in the
Planning & Development Department, Public Works Department, and Department of
Parks, Recreation & Waterfront to provide information, coordination, and analysis for the
East Bay Crescent Sub-Regional Vulnerability Assessment and Shoreline Adaptation
Planning project.
sections.current_situation:
The California Ocean Protection Council (OPC) has awarded the Association of Bay
Area Governments (ABAG)/San Francisco Estuary Partnership (SFEP), in partnership
with the Cities of Berkeley, Albany, Emeryville and Oakland, $2,240,000 over two and a
half years for the East Bay Crescent (EPC) Sub-Regional Vulnerability Assessment and
Shoreline Adaptation Planning Project. Of the total grant amount, the City of Berkeley
will receive $223,151 over the grant period. ABAG/SFEP will serve as the prime
recipient for this funding opportunity, and the City of Berkeley will be a subrecipient to
ABAG/SFEP.
2180 Milvia Street, Berkeley, CA 94704 ● Tel: (510) 981-7000 ● TDD: (510) 981-6903 ● Fax: (510) 981-7099
E-Mail: CManager@berkeleyca.gov Website: http://www.BerkeleyCA.gov/Manager

Accept SB1 Sea Level Adaptation Grant Funds, CONSENT CALENDAR
and contract with ABAG/SF Estuary Project February 24, 2026
The EBC Sub-Regional Vulnerability Assessment and Shoreline Adaptation Planning
will advance sea-level rise adaptation along the shorelines of Albany, Berkeley,
Emeryville, and a portion of Oakland, from the Bay Bridge/Radio Beach to Cerritos
Creek. A unique collaboration will result in a coordinated approach to adaptation in the
EBC. Key components include development of an EBC Working Group and Equity
Committee that will result in a sub-regional vulnerability assessment, with sub-regionally
coordinated, but distinct, shoreline adaptation plans for the City of Albany, Berkeley,
and Emeryville that adhere to the SF Bay Conservation and Development
Commission’s (BCDC) Regional Shoreline Adaptation Plan (RSAP) guidelines.
The work that will be completed under this grant supports the City’s Strategic Plan goal
to be a global leader in addressing climate change, advancing environmental justice,
and protecting the environment.
sections.background:
In 2021, Governor Newsom signed Senate Bill 1 (Atkins, 2021)1 into law. Senate Bill 1
(SB 1) directs the state to provide funding to local and regional governments to develop
sea level rise (SLR) adaptation plans and implementation projects. OPC’s SB 1 SLR
Adaptation Planning Grant Program (SB 1 Grant Program) aims to provide funding for
coastal communities to develop consistent SLR adaptation plans and projects to build
resilience to SLR along the entire coast of California and San Francisco Bay.
In 2023, the California State Legislature passed SB 272 (Laird), Sea level rise: planning
and adaptation. SB 272 addressed the need for coordinated standardized sea level rise
adaptation by requiring local governments along the San Francisco Bay shoreline to
develop Subregional Shoreline Adaptation Plans. Bay Area Subregional Shoreline
Adaptation Plans are required to meet the Guidelines established by BCDC in the
RSAP, ensuring effective and collaborative regional responses.
ENVIRONMENTAL SUSTAINABILITY AND CLIMATE IMPACTS
This funding will provide valuable technical information around the sea level rise
vulnerabilities that exist in the regional East Bay Crescent area, which includes
Berkeley, and will provide additional planning support and information to understand
how the City and community can best plan for and mitigate these climate impacts.
sections.rationale:
Accepting this grant funding would allow the City to advance necessary work to
understand shoreline vulnerabilities and ensure that community priorities drive

RESOLUTION NO. ##,###-N.S.
RESOLUTION TO ACCEPT GRANT FUNDS FROM THE OCEAN PROTECTION
COUNCIL’S SB1 SEA LEVEL RISE ADAPTATION PLANNING GRANT PROGRAM,
AND CONTRACT WITH THE ASSOCIATION OF BAY AREA GOVERNMENTS/SAN
FRANCISCO ESTUARY PARTNERSHIP FOR THE EAST BAY CRESCENT SUB-
REGIONAL VULNERABILITY ASSESSMENT AND SHORELINE ADAPTATION
PLANNING
WHEREAS, climate change is accelerating rising sea levels, increasing storm frequency
and intensity, and moving groundwater toward the surface. The confluence of more
intense winter storms, extreme high tides, and higher runoff, with higher sea levels, will
increase the frequency and duration of shoreline flooding long before areas are
permanently inundated by sea level rise alone; and
WHEREAS, the San Francisco Bay Area is a vibrant, diverse, ecologically unique,
innovative, and pioneering region that will be deeply and deleteriously affected by climate
change without tremendous effort and investments to adapt to a constantly changing
shoreline. The San Francisco Bay shoreline constitutes approximately one-third of the
California coastline, but the Bay Area is estimated to experience two-thirds of the negative
economic impacts due to the flooding that would occur absent adequate measures to
adapt and protect people, places, and habitat; and
WHEREAS, the Bay Area region’s most socioeconomically vulnerable frontline
communities are at the greatest risk of exposure to climate threats, and the impacts of
historic and ongoing social and economic marginalization will compound the risks posed
by flooding to those communities by reducing a community’s or individual’s ability to
prepare for, respond to, and/or recover from a flood event; and
WHEREAS, flood damage to vital shoreline development, public infrastructure, and
facilities such as neighborhoods, commercial centers, a
